content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CorvlrvussloN
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
April 29, 1999
Gregory N. Weiler
Pal.mieri, Tyler, Wiener, Wilhelm & Waidron LLP
2603 Main Street
East Tower - Suite 1300
Irvine, California 92614-6228
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-060
Dear Mr. Weiler:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mr. William Jensen, a
city councilmember for the City ofHesperia, regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
You also asked some questions regarding Government Code Section 1090, which is not
under the jurisdiction ofthe Act. Please contact the Office of the Attorney General for questions
regarding Section 1090.
QUESTIONS
1) May Mr. Jensen participate in the City ofHesperia’s decision to acquire two
properties, one owned by the K&K Partnership and the other owned by the Jorad Partnership,
located at the intersection ofInterstate 15 and Main Street within the city, by either negotiated
purchase or eminent domain?
2) May Mr. Jensen participate in decisions regarding consulting contracts, i.e.
preliminary appraisals and engineering work, relating to the acquisition of these properties?
3) Will the result be the same ifMr. Jensen receives the same commission from the
sellers regardless of whether the properties are purchased or condemned?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code ofRegulations.


File No. A-99-060
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1) Mr. Jensen must disqualify himself and he may not participate in the decision
regarding the acquisition of the property.
2) Mi. Jensen must disqualify himself and he may not participate in the decisions
regarding consulting contracts relating to the acquisition ofthe properties.
3) Yes.
FACTS
Mr. Jensen is a real estate broker and owner of International Key West Realty, a sole
proprietorship. In 1993, at least five years before his election to the city council, Mr. Jensen
entered into real estate listing agreements with the owners of two properties at the intersection of
Interstate 15 and Main Street within the City ofHesperia (“properties”), which listings are
maintained to date. One property is owned by K&K Partnership; the other property is owned by
the Jorad Partnership. Each listing provides for a 6 percent commission to Mr. Jensen, as the
broker for the sellers ofthe properties. He also has an agreement with both sellers that he will
receive a 6 percent commission if the properties are condemned.
The City ofHesperia and the California Department of Transportation (“CalTrans”) will
acquire a portion ofthe properties either by negotiated sale or by eminent domain.
Mr. Jensen also has other listings with both of these partnerships as clients; none ofthe
listings are in escrow. Neither partnership has been a source of $250 or more in income in the
past 12 months.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)
A public official hasa “financial interest” in a governmental decision, within the meaning
ofthe Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more ofthe public official’s economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis for deciding whether
a public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b).) The following advice applies that eight-step analysis to your question
regarding whether Mr. Jensen may participate in the above decisions.


File No. A-99-060
Page 3
1. Public Official.
As a member of the City ofHesperia City Council, Mr. Jensen is a public official under
the Act. (Section 82048.)
2. Making, participating in making or using his or her official position to influence
governmental decisions.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when the public official “make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his/her officialposition to influence a
governmental decision in which he/she knows or has reason to know he/she has a financial
interest.” (Section 87100, emphasis added.)
The Commission has adopted a series of
regulations which define “making,” “participating in making,” and “influencing” a governmental
decision. (Regulations 18702-18702.4, copies enclosed.)
Therefore, the scope ofMr. Jensen’s participation may be broader than just voting on the
matter.
3. Identifying Councilmember Jensen’s economic interests.
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. (Section 87103.) The “economic interests” from which conflicts of interest may arise
are defined in Section 87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5. Section 87103 provides as
follows:
“A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 ifit is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member ofhis
or her immediate family, or on any ofthe following:
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,


File No. A-99-060
Page 4
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. The
amount of the value of gifts specified by this subdivision shall be
adjusted biennially by the commission to equal the same amount
determined by the commission pursuant to subdivision (f) of
Section 89503.
For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means
any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child
of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.”
Under Regulation 18703 .3(c)(3)(B), the sources of commission income for a real estate
broker include:
“(i) The person the broker represents in the transaction;
(ii) Ifthe broker receives a commission from a transaction
conducted by an agent working under the broker’s auspices, the
person represented by the agent;
(iii) Any brokerage business entity through which the broker
conducts business; and
(iv) Any person who receives a finder’s or other referral fee for
referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a
referral pursuant to a contract with the broker.”
Therefore, pursuant to the regulation above, Mr. Jensen has two economic interests which
will be affected by the decision: (i) the partnerships/sellers he represents in the transaction who
have listed the properties with him, and (iii) his real estate business, which is a sole
proprietorship. (Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).)
Neither partnership/seller has been a source of income of $250 or more in the past twelve
months preceding the decision. “Promised income,” within the meaning of Section 87103(c),
may also be a disqualifying financial interest. We have advised that commission income is
deemed promised income to a real estate broker when a sale is pending (i.e. the property is in
escrow). Thus, having a listing on a property does not constitute promised income.
However,
once a sale is pending on a property, whether or not that property is the subject of the decision,
the client is deemed to be a source ofpromised income. (Clay Advice Letter, No. 1-96-335,
Bergman Advice Letter, No. A-93-297, Leichter Advice Letter, No. 1-98-173.)


File No. A-99-060
Page 5
The rationale that having a listing on a property does not constitute promised income is
that a listing is too speculative to rise to the level ofpromised income. (Felts Advice Letter,
No. A-85- 130.2) However, once escrow is opened, there is a buyer and a seller, and the element
of speculation has diminished.
In this situation, Mr. Jensen has been the exclusive agent for both ofthe sellers for five
years. The city plans to acquire the two properties necessary for the project; the only decision is
as to how the properties will be acquired. Since the properties will be acquired either by a
negotiated sale or by eminent domain, there is no element of speculation as to whether
Mr. Jensen will receive promised income. Therefore, based on the very narrow facts of this
situation, even though the properties are not in escrow, we would consider that there is promised
income since the city plans to acquire both properties and the only issue, which is the subject of
the governmental decision, is how the properties will be acquired. Accordingly, if any source of
income or promised income will be materially affected by the decision, Mr. Jensen may not
participate in the decision.
4. Are the economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Determining whether each ofthe public official’s economic interests is directly or
indirectly involved in the governmental decision, (Regulation 1 8700(b)(4)), is important because
it helps determine which materiality test to use in deciding whether it is reasonably foreseeab1e
that the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on the economic interest. A
business entity is directly involved in a decision when it:
•
Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request (Regulation 18704.1 (a)(1));
•
Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency. A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding
if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any
license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).)
Both the K&K Partnership and the Jorad Partnership are directly involved in the decision
because the property that each partnership owns is the subject of the decision. (Regulation
18704.1(b), Whitney Advice Letter, No. A-99-077.) The city council will decide how to acquire
the property, either by negotiated sale or by condemnation.
Therefore, for purposes ofthis analysis, the sources ofpromised income are directly
involved and Mr. Jensen’s economic interest in his real estate company is indirectly involved in
the decision.
2 This letter was superseded in part on the issue of multiple listings not constituting promised income,
Bergman Advice Letter, supra. That issue is not relevant in this analysis.


File No. A-99-060
Page 6
S. What is the appropriate materiality standard?
(a) International Key West Realty.
Mr. Jensen’s real estate company is a business entity which is indirectly involved in the
decision. Regulation 18705.1(b) establishes monetary thresholds to determine when a decision
has a material financial effect on a business entity. For small business entities, Regulation
18705.l(b)(7) sets the fiscal standards. You stated that this is the correct section to apply to
Mr. Jensen’s business and that Mr. Jensen anticipates receipt ofmore than $10,000 in
commission income upon the sale or condemnation ofthe two properties. Therefore, the
governmental decision to acquire the properties will have a material financial effect on his
business. Mr. Jensen also may not participate in any decisions regarding consulting contracts
relating to the acquisition ofthe properties.
(b) Sources ofpromised income: K&K Partnership and the Jorad Partnership, the
owners/sellers of the properties.
The sources of income are directly involved in the acquisition decision since it is their
properties that are the subject of the decision. Since the financial effect on a directly involved
source of income is deemed material, Mr. Jensen may not participate in the decision to acquire
the properties. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation 18706.) When the effect of a decision is deemed material under the
appropriate materiality regulation, the public official must determine whether it is substantially
likely that any financial effect will occur as a result ofthe decision--if any financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable, the official will have a disqualif’ing financial interest. (Regulation
1 8700(b)(6).)
A decision for the city to acquire the properties by either purchase or eminent domain
will have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Mr. Jensen. He is the exclusive broker for
both properties and he will benefit by receiving a commission in either kind oftransaction.
Steps 7 and 8
These steps analyze the public generally exception and whether the official’s participation
is legally required. Neither are relevant to your questions.


File No. A-99-060
Page 7
Disqualification.
When a public official is disqualified, he/she must disclose the financial interest as part of
the agency’s official record. (Regulation 18702.1 (a)(5).)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
jtecI6t14D
S
Counsel, Legal Division

conducts business; and

referring a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a

regarding the acquisition of the property.
